narrative:
I'm sorry to deliver this news, but your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates an 81% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, based on patterns from thousands of past students. You have a solid background in some areas, like coming from an upper-middle-income family and planning to study full-time, but your academic record raised significant concerns. Specifically, your grades placed you in the 2nd decile in both your first and third years of school—much lower than the typical student's 5th or 6th decile position. Your LSAT score of 25 is also well below the average of about 37, and your undergraduate GPA of 2.50 falls short of the usual 3.21. As a female Asian student, you're in a smaller group compared to the mostly White, male applicants who make up the majority of the dataset, but these demographics played a minor role here.

The model's decision was driven primarily by your third-year school decile ranking, which had the strongest influence in pushing the prediction toward failure. Right behind that was your LSAT score, which further tipped the scales against you, followed closely by your first-year decile. Your family income and racial background had the next biggest impacts, both nudging toward a failure prediction, while your undergraduate GPA contributed somewhat less but still in the same direction. Your full-time study plan had almost no effect, and being female actually slightly favored a passing prediction, though it wasn't enough to outweigh the others.

In comparison to typical students who pass the bar, your low decile rankings and subpar test scores stand out as major deviations, signaling potential struggles with the rigorous demands ahead. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just how your profile aligns with historical outcomes. To improve your chances next time, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to boost that score toward the average or higher, as it's a key factor you can directly improve with preparation. Strengthening your overall academic performance through targeted study habits could also help lift future decile rankings or equivalent metrics. Consider gaining more experience or credentials that demonstrate resilience, and reapply once you've addressed these core areas—many students turn things around with focused effort. We're rooting for your success.
